title: What Artificial Intelligence Actually Is
body:
Artificial intelligence, as a field of study, began with a deceptively simple question: can machines think? Alan Turing posed this question in 1950, proposing what became known as the Turing Test — if a machine can converse with a human so convincingly that the human cannot reliably tell whether they are speaking to a person or a machine, can we say it is intelligent? This framing has been debated for decades, but it set the stage for a discipline that has grown from a handful of academics at a 1956 Dartmouth conference into one of the most consequential areas of research and engineering in the world.

The early decades of AI were dominated by symbolic approaches — the idea that intelligence could be captured by encoding knowledge as logical rules and having machines manipulate those symbols according to formal logic. Expert systems of the 1970s and 1980s attempted to replicate the decision-making of human specialists by encoding thousands of if-then rules. These systems worked in narrow domains — diagnosing certain diseases, configuring computer systems — but proved brittle when faced with the messiness and ambiguity of the real world. They could not handle situations their rules did not anticipate, and building the rule sets required enormous manual effort.

The connectionist revolution changed everything. Rather than programming rules explicitly, researchers built artificial neural networks — layers of simple computational units loosely inspired by biological neurons — and trained them to learn patterns directly from data. The key insight was that by adjusting the strength of connections between units (called weights or parameters), a network could be trained to approximate arbitrarily complex functions. The backpropagation algorithm, popularised in the 1980s and refined over decades, provided an efficient way to compute how each weight should change to reduce the network's errors. But it was not until the 2010s, when massive datasets and powerful GPUs became available, that deep learning — neural networks with many layers — began achieving superhuman performance on tasks like image classification, speech recognition, and game playing.

Modern AI is built on several key paradigms that often work together. Supervised learning trains models on labelled examples — pairs of inputs and correct outputs — and is the workhorse behind classification, regression, and prediction tasks. Unsupervised learning finds structure in unlabelled data, used for clustering, dimensionality reduction, and increasingly for pre-training foundation models on massive text corpora. Reinforcement learning trains agents to take actions in an environment to maximise a reward signal, and has produced landmark results from AlphaGo's defeat of the world Go champion to training large language models to produce helpful and harmless responses.

What AI systems can do today is genuinely remarkable but also genuinely limited. Large language models can write coherent essays, translate between languages, summarise documents, generate code, answer complex questions, and engage in multi-turn conversations that feel remarkably human. Computer vision systems can identify objects, faces, and scenes with extraordinary accuracy. AI systems can generate photorealistic images, compose music, and predict protein structures. But these systems do not understand what they are doing in the way humans understand things. They do not have goals, desires, beliefs, or consciousness. They are sophisticated mathematical functions that have been optimised to produce outputs that correlate with human expectations.

The distinction between narrow AI and artificial general intelligence (AGI) is crucial. Every AI system deployed today is narrow — designed and trained for specific tasks or domains. Even the most capable large language models, which can handle a remarkable range of tasks, are fundamentally operating within the distribution of their training data. AGI, by contrast, would be a system with the kind of flexible, general-purpose intelligence that humans possess — the ability to learn any intellectual task, reason about novel situations, transfer knowledge across domains, and adapt to entirely new challenges. Whether AGI is achievable with current approaches, or requires fundamental breakthroughs we have not yet imagined, is one of the most debated questions in the field. Serious researchers hold positions ranging from 'it could happen within a decade' to 'we have no idea how to build it and may never figure it out.'
keyPoints: AI is a broad field encompassing symbolic reasoning, statistical learning, neural networks, and hybrid approaches — not a single technology
Modern AI is dominated by deep learning, which uses multi-layer neural networks trained on large datasets to learn representations and make predictions
The transformer architecture, introduced in 2017, is the foundation of virtually all frontier language models and many vision systems
Supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning are the three primary paradigms, each suited to different problem types
Current AI systems are narrow — they excel at specific trained tasks but lack general-purpose reasoning and true understanding
The history of AI includes several 'winters' where progress stalled and funding dried up, followed by breakthroughs that reignited the field
Large language models achieve remarkable performance through next-token prediction at scale, not through explicit knowledge representation
The gap between narrow AI and artificial general intelligence remains vast and poorly characterised — it is not simply a matter of building bigger models
furtherReading: 'Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach' by Stuart Russell and Peter Norvig — the definitive textbook covering the full breadth of AI
Anthropic's research blog — interpretability, alignment, and responsible AI development
Google DeepMind's publications — reinforcement learning, AlphaFold, and frontier capabilities research
'The Alignment Problem' by Brian Christian — accessible overview of AI safety and value alignment challenges
